The identity-component construction extracts the open-and-closed

In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

220	
221	1. Read your directive completely.
222	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.

For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
227	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set$ of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	$C/k$ projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	$\Hilb_{C/k}$ and its open subscheme $\Div_{C/k}$ of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective $k$-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for $\Pic$ assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$. The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on $C \times_k T$ to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor $D \subset C \times_k T$ over $T$
64	(an $S$-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, hence a $T$-point of the Hilbert
66	functor $\Hilb_{C/k}(T)$, which is the special case $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of $\IHilb_{X/S}$); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat.  Then $\Div_{X/S}$ is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme $\IDiv_{X/S}$ of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  $\IHilb_{X/S}$.
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      $$A_{X/S}(T)\:\Div_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)$$
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ to the
92	  %  sheaf $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)$.  The target $\Pic_{X/S}$ may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where $\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}$).}
97	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  $T$ be a $k$-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \Div_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where $\Div_{C/k}(T)$ is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor $\Div_{C/k}$ is representable
107	  by an open subscheme $\Div_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}$ of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with $\Hilb_{C/k}$ the special case
109	  $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of $k$-schemes $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set $H:=\IHilb_{X/S}$, and let $W\subset X\x H$ be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and $q\:W\to H$ the projection.  Let $V$ denote the
118	% set of points $w\in W$ at which $W$ is an effective divisor. Plainly $V$
119	% is open in $W$.  Set $Z:=q(W-V)$.  Then $Z$ is closed because $q$ is
120	% proper.  Set $U:=H-Z$.  Then $U$ is open, and $q^{-1}U$ is an effective
121	% divisor in $X\x U$.  In fact, since $q$ is flat, $q^{-1}U$ is a relative
122	% effective divisor in $X\x U/U$.
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ is representable by a $k$-scheme; this uses
128	  $C/k$ projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let $W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}$ be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and $q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}$ the projection. The locus
131	  $V \subseteq W$ where $W$ is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement $W \setminus V$
133	  is closed in $W$, and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}$ is closed because $q$ is proper. Hence $\Div_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z$ is open in $\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on $\Div_{C/k}$ the
137	  restriction of $W$ is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \Div_{C/k} / \Div_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$
143	  to the invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in $T$: pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along $\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$
147	  takes the ideal of $D$ to the ideal of $D_{T'}$ (since $D$ is flat over
148	  $T$, the schematic inverse image $D_{T'}$ is a relative effective
149	  divisor on $C \times_k T'/T'$, again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})$.
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map $A_{C/k}(T) : \Div_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
156	\end{proof}

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: $S = \Spec k$, $X = C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	$X/S$ projective Zariski-locally over $S$, flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence,
173	        thm:quot_representable, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
174	        thm:pullback_natural}
175	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
176	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to $S = \Spec k$, $X$ complete);
177	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
178	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
179	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
180	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
181	  %   Assume $f\:X\to S$ is projective Zariski locally over $S$, and is flat
182	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
183	  %
184	  %  \tu{(1)} Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, is separated and locally of finite
185	  %  type over $S$, and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.
186	  %
187	  %  \tu{(2)} If also $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective, then
188	  %  $\IPic_{X/S}$ is a disjoint union of open
189	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
190	  %  $S$-schemes.
191	  %   \end{thm}"
192	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
193	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
194	  %    Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$, and $X$ is complete.  Then
195	  %  $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists, represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$, and is a disjoint
196	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
197	  %   \end{sbscor}"
198	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
199	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
200	  Let $k$ be a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
201	  over $k$. Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
202	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
203	  representable by a $k$-scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, separated and locally of
204	  finite type over $k$, which is a disjoint union of open
205	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
206	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ is called the
207	  \emph{Picard scheme} of $C/k$.
208	\end{theorem}
209	
210	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
211	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
212	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
213	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
214	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on $S$ to
215	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of $S$-schemes.  Moreover, it
216	% is also a local matter on $S$ to prove that an $S$-scheme is separated
217	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
218	% $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective.
219	% [\dots]
220	% Plainly an $S$-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
221	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
222	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
223	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
224	\begin{proof}
225	  \uses{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence, thm:quot_representable,
226	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:pullback_natural,
227	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
228	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to $S = \Spec k$ and
229	  $X = C$. The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
230	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
231	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
232	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
233	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
234	
235	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
236	  For each polynomial $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$, let $P^\phi \subseteq
237	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ be the \'etale subsheaf whose $T$-points are
238	  represented by invertible sheaves $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ with
239	  $\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)$ for all $t \in T$ (the
240	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
241	  $\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on the projective curve $C$). The $P^\phi$ form a
242	  disjoint open cover of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (openness via Kleiman's
243	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
244	  represent each $P^\phi$ by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
245	  $k$-schemes.
246	
247	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by $m$-regularity.}
248	  Given $\phi$, fix $m$ large enough that the $T$-points of $P^\phi$ are
249	  representable by $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ satisfying
250	  $R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0$ for all $i \ge 1$, $n \ge m$. Twisting
251	  by $\mathcal{O}_C(m)$ defines an automorphism of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
252	  that carries $P^\phi$ to $P^{\phi_0}_0$ for $\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)$.
253	  Hence it suffices to represent each $P^{\phi_0}_0$ by a quasi-projective
254	  $k$-scheme.
255	
256	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
257	  Consider the Abel map $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
258	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source $\Div_{C/k}$
259	  is representable by an open subscheme of $\Hilb_{C/k}$ (and hence by an
260	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
261	  is quasi-projective over $k$. Pulling back along
262	  $P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ yields an open
263	  subscheme $Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \Div_{C/k}$
264	  of $\Div_{C/k}$, which is again quasi-projective. The projection
265	  $\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
266	  given a $T$-point $\lambda$ of $P^{\phi_0}_0$ represented by an \'etale
267	  cover $T' \to T$ and an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}'$ on $C \times_k
268	  T'$ with $H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
269	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'$ associated to
270	  $\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'$ is smooth over $T'$; an \'etale
271	  cover $T_1 \to T'$ admits a $T'$-map $T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})$
272	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
273	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z$ produces the required $T_1$-point of $Z$.
274	  Moreover, the first projection $Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z$ is
275	  smooth and proper.
276	
277	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
278	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection $\alpha : Z \to P$
279	  of \'etale sheaves with $Z$ quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$
280	  representable, and the first projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper,
281	  $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
282	  $P = P^{\phi_0}_0$, $Z$ and $R$ as above, so $P^{\phi_0}_0$ is
283	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$ and
284	  over $m$, the disjoint union of representable $P^\phi$ pieces represents
285	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
286	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
287	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the $S = \Spec k$,
288	  $X = C$ complete case.
289	\end{proof}
290	
291	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
292	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
293	
294	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
295	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
296	abstract content allowing the descent from $Z = \Div^{\phi_0}_0$ to the
297	representing Picard piece.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}\leanok
300	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
303	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
304	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
305	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
306	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
307	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
308	  %   Let $\alpha\:Z\to P$ be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set $R:=Z\x_PZ$.
309	  %  Assume $\alpha$ is a surjection, $Z$ is representable by a
310	  %  quasi-projective $S$-scheme, $R$ is representable by an $S$-scheme, and
311	  %  the first projection $R\to Z$ is representable by a smooth and proper
312	  %  map.  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $S$-scheme, and
313	  %  $\alpha$ is representable by a smooth map."
314	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
315	  Let $\alpha : Z \to P$ be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)$.
316	  Set $R := Z \times_P Z$. Suppose:
317	  \begin{enumerate}
318	    \item $\alpha$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
319	    \item $Z$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme;
320	    \item $R$ is representable by a $k$-scheme;
321	    \item the first projection $R \to Z$ is representable by a smooth
322	      and proper map.
323	  \end{enumerate}
324	  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme, and $\alpha$
325	  is representable by a smooth map.
326	\end{lemma}
327	
328	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
329	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
330	% structural heart of step 4.)
331	% "To lighten the notation, let $Z$ and $R$ denote the
332	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map $Z\to S$ is
333	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
334	% $Z\x_SZ\to Z$ is too.  But the first projection $R\to Z$ is proper, and
335	% factors naturally through a map $h\:R\to Z\x_SZ$.  Hence $h$ is proper.
336	% But $h$ is a monomorphism; that is, $h$ is injective on $T$-points.
337	% Therefore, $h$ is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
338	%
339	% Plainly, for each $S$-scheme $T$, the subset $R(T)\subset
340	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
341	% map of schemes $R\to Z$ is flat and proper, and $Z$ is a
342	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
343	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat and projective map
344	% $Z\to Q$ such that $R=Z\x_QZ$.  (In other words, a flat and proper
345	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
346	% [\dots]
347	% Since $Z\to Q$ is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
348	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
349	% same as those of $R\to Z$.  And $R\to Z$ is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
350	% $Z\to Q$ is smooth.
351	%
352	% It remains to see that $Q$ represents $P$.  First, observe that $Z\to Q$
353	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
354	% [\dots]
355	% Since $Z\to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, $Q$ is, in the
356	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
357	% $R\rightrightarrows Z$ by Exercise~\ref{ex:epi} below, which is an
358	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
359	% exercise, $P$ too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
360	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus $Q$
361	% represents $P$."
362	\begin{proof}
363	  By (ii) the structure map $Z \to \Spec k$ is quasi-projective, hence
364	  separated, so the first projection $Z \times_k Z \to Z$ is separated.
365	  By (iv) the first projection $R \to Z$ is proper; it factors through
366	  $h : R \to Z \times_k Z$ over the diagonal embedding (the second
367	  component is the second projection of $R \to Z \times_P Z$). The map
368	  $h$ is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because $R$ is the graph
369	  of an equivalence relation on $Z$, hence injective on $T$-points).
370	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: $h$ is a
371	  closed embedding.
372	
373	  Hence $R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z$ is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
374	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$. By the
375	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
376	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
377	  the graph $R \subseteq Z \times_k Z$ inside the universal subscheme of
378	  the Hilbert scheme $\Hilb_{Z/k}$ and descends to a closed subscheme
379	  $Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}$ representing the quotient), there exist a
380	  quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat, projective map
381	  $Z \to Q$ with $R = Z \times_Q Z$.
382	
383	  The map $Z \to Q$ is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
384	  extension, coincide with those of $R \to Z$, which are smooth by
385	  hypothesis. Hence $Z \to Q$ is smooth.
386	
387	  Finally, $Z \to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
388	  cover of any $T$-point of $Q$ pulls back to a $T'$-point of $Z$, by
389	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to $Z \times_Q T \to T$). In the category
390	  of \'etale sheaves, both $Q$ and $P$ are coequalisers of the pair
391	  $R \rightrightarrows Z$; the coequaliser is unique up to unique
392	  isomorphism, so $Q$ represents $P$. Smoothness of $\alpha : Z \to P$
393	  is the smoothness of $Z \to Q$ just shown.
394	\end{proof}

396	\section{Group-scheme structure}
397	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
398	
399	The Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ inherits an abelian group structure from
400	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on $C \times_k T$. The relative
401	Picard presheaf $T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T$ is already an abelian
402	group at every $T$ (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
403	functorially in $T$; this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
404	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
405

411	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
412	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
413	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
414	  % text (group-scheme structure on $\IPic_{X/S}$ via tensor product);
415	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
416	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
418	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}

434	  Let $\Pic_{C/k}$ be the Picard scheme of
435	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then $\Pic_{C/k}$ is canonically an
436	  abelian group scheme over $k$: the tensor product
437	  $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]$
438	  and inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$ on
439	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ (well defined because both operations

445	  $e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}$ (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying

451	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in

462	  \mathcal{M}]$, inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
463	  $0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]$; these operations descend through
464	  the quotient by $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ because $\pi_T^*$ is itself a

480	  inverse) hold at the level of $T$-points functorially in $T$, hence

482	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
483	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, $k$ denotes a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ of relative dimension $1$. In other words, $C \to \Spec k$ is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$; see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$ is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the $k$-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of $C$.
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-$k$-modules construction and applying the $k$-linear sheaf cohomology $H^1$. The result is a $k$-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's $\ModuleCat k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier $\toModuleKSheaf C$ is the sheaf-of-$k$-modules constructed from $\struct C$ via the structure morphism; the wrapper $\HModule k F 1$ is the $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$, which carries a canonical $\Module k$-structure inherited from the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ returns the $k$-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme guarantees $\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty$, so $g(C)$ is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item $g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})$ (Serre duality).
45	  \item $g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)$, where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over $\mathbb C$, $g(C)$ equals the topological genus of $C(\mathbb C)$.
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition $\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ requires a $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of $\struct C$ on the topology of opens of the underlying space of $C$. Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\AddCommGrpCat$-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)$.
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel $\Module k$-valued construction with unit object $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$ instead of $\ULift\,\mathbb Z$, gluing through the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a $\Module k$ structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaf} (this project): for $C$ over $\Spec k$, the sheafification of the presheaf $U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))$ where the $k$-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{$H^0$ algebraic bridge} (this project): $H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)$, via Mathlib's $\Ext$ linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content $H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k$ on a connected proper $k$-curve.
60	\end{itemize}

2836	\section{The connected component of the identity}\label{sc:Pic0}
2837	 Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$, we now
2838	turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
2839	$\IPicz_{X/S}$ of the connected components of the identity element,
2840	$\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$, for $s\in S$.  We establish a number of basic
2841	properties, especially when $S$ is the spectrum of a field.
2842	
2843	It is remarkable how much we can prove about $\IPicz_{X/S}$ formally, or
2844	nearly so, from general principles.  Notably, we can do without the
2845	finiteness theorems proved in the next section.  In order to emphasize
2846	the formal nature and corresponding generality of the arguments,
2847	most of the results are stated with the general hypothesis that
2848	$\IPic_{X/S}$ exists instead of with specific hypotheses that imply it
2849	exists.
2850	
2851	\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
2852	 Let $k$ be a field, and $G$ a group scheme locally of finite
2853	type.  Let $G^0$ denote the connected component of the identity element $e$.
2854	
2855	\tu{(1)} Then $G$ is separated.
2856	
2857	\tu{(2)} Then $G$ is smooth if it has a geometrically
2858	reduced open subscheme.
2859	
2860	 \tu{(3)} Then $G^0$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
2861	type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
2862	extending $k$.
2863	 \end{lem}
2864	 \begin{proof}
2865	 Since $e$ is a $k$-point, it is closed.  Define a map $\alpha\:G\x G\to
2866	G$ on $T$-points by $\alpha(g,h):=gh^{-1}$.  Then $\alpha^{-1}e\subset
2867	G\x G$ is a closed subscheme.  Its $T$-points are just the pairs $(g,g)$
2868	for $g\in G(T)$; so $\alpha^{-1}e$ is the diagonal.  Thus (1) holds.
2869	
2870	Suppose $G$ has a geometrically reduced open subscheme $V$.  To prove

\chapter{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
\label{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-184). This chapter is the A.3 sub-row of Route~A
% (positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It builds the
% identity component $\Picz_{C/k}$ of the Picard scheme as an
% open-and-closed subgroup scheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$, equips it with the
% degree-zero characterisation, and identifies it as an abelian variety
% of dimension $g(C)$. The abstract identity-component substrate
% (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} +
% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is new project material:
% the Lean target
% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} does not yet
% exist in Mathlib.
%
% NOTE: future split candidate when a second consumer materialises. The
% abstract identity-component substrate is kept in this chapter (rather
% than split off into a separate
% \texttt{GroupScheme\_IdentityComponent.tex}) because it is small and
% has a single consumer for now. If a second consumer materialises,
% revisit the split.

% NOTE (planner directive iter-184). The Lean target file
% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean} does not yet
% exist; this chapter is the authoritative specification, and the Lean
% skeleton is owed in a follow-up iteration.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:pic0_setup}

Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper
geometrically integral curve $C$ over $k$ of genus $g = g(C)$
(\cref{def:genus}). By
\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of $C/k$ is represented by
the $k$-group scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
separated, locally of finite type over $k$, and a disjoint union of
open quasi-projective $k$-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
$\Pic_{C/k}$ stratifies its $T$-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
degree of a line bundle.

The identity component $\Picz_{C/k}$ singled out by Kleiman is the open
and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
$\Pic_{C/k}$ containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
abelian variety of dimension $g$ --- the Jacobian variety of $C$. This
chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.

\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}

The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
Lean substrate
\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
outside the Picard context.

\begin{definition}[Identity component of a group scheme]
  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
  %  Let $k$ be a field, and $G$ a group scheme locally of finite
  % type.  Let $G^0$ denote the connected component of the identity element $e$.
  %
  % \tu{(1)} Then $G$ is separated.
  %
  % \tu{(2)} Then $G$ is smooth if it has a geometrically
  % reduced open subscheme.
  %
  %  \tu{(3)} Then $G^0$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
  % extending $k$.
  %  \end{lem}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
  Let $k$ be a field and $G$ a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type
  whose identity section $e : \Spec k \to G$ factors through $G$. The
  \emph{identity component} of $G$, denoted $G^0$, is the locally closed
  subscheme of $G$ whose underlying topological space is the connected
  component of $|G|$ containing the image of $e$, equipped with the
  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from $G$.
  In characteristic zero, or more generally when $G$ has a
  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Identity component is an open subgroup scheme of finite type]
  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
  \uses{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % " \tu{(3)} Then $G^0$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
  % extending $k$."
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
  Let $k$ be a field and $G$ a $k$-group scheme locally of finite
  type. Then the identity component $G^0$
  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
  subscheme of $G$, the open immersion $G^0 \hookrightarrow G$ is a
  homomorphism of $k$-group schemes (in particular $G^0$ inherits a
  $k$-group-scheme structure from $G$), $G^0$ is of finite type over
  $k$ and geometrically irreducible, and the formation of $G^0$
  commutes with extension of the base field: for any field extension
  $K/k$, the natural map $(G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0$ is an isomorphism.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
% (3).)
% "Consider (3).  By definition, $G^0$ is the largest connected subspace
% containing $e$.  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
% connected; so $G^0$ is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
% $G^0$ is open too.
%
% Since $G^0$ is connected and has a $k$-point, $G^0$ is geometrically
% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming $G^0$ commutes with
% extending $k$.
%
% Furthermore, $G^0\x G^0$ is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
% So $\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G$ is connected, and contains $e$,
% so lies in $G^0$.  Thus $G^0$ is a subgroup.
%
% To prove $G^0$ is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
% replace $k$ by its algebraic closure.  Then $G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}$ is
% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence $\alpha$ induces a map from
% $G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}$ into $G^0_{\red}$.  So $G^0_{\red}$ is a
% subgroup.  Thus we may replace $G^0$ by $G^0_{\red}$.
% [\dots]
% Hence $G^0$ is irreducible locally at $h$.  But $h$ is
% arbitrary, and $G^0$ is connected.  So $G^0$ is irreducible by
% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
% [\dots]
% Now, $U\x U$ is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence $G^0$ is
% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, $G$ is locally of finite type. Hence
% $G^0$ is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
  Since $e$ is a $k$-rational point, the singleton $\{e\}$ is a closed
  point of $G$. The connected component $G^0$ of $|G|$ at $e$ is the
  largest connected subspace containing $e$; the closure of a connected
  subspace is connected, hence $G^0$ is closed. On the other hand, a
  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
  (EGA I 6.1.9), so $G^0 \subseteq |G|$ is also open. Equip $G^0$ with
  the open-subscheme structure.

  Geometric connectedness of $G^0$ follows from the existence of the
  $k$-rational point $e \in G^0(k)$ (EGA IV$_2$ 4.5.14); in
  particular the formation of $G^0$ commutes with extension of the
  base field. The product $G^0 \times_k G^0$ is again connected
  (EGA IV$_2$ 4.5.8). Let $\alpha : G \times_k G \to G$,
  $\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}$, be the algebraic-group structure map.
  Since $\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)$ is connected and contains $e$, its
  image lies in $G^0$. Hence $G^0$ is closed under the group operation
  (and under inversion, by taking $g = e$): the inclusion
  $G^0 \hookrightarrow G$ is a homomorphism of $k$-group schemes.

  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
  to $\overline k$ (which is permitted because formation of $G^0$
  commutes with field extension). Over $\overline k$, the product
  $G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}$ is reduced (EGA IV$_2$ 4.6.1), so
  $G^0_{\red}$ is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace $G^0$ by
  $G^0_{\red}$ for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme $U \subseteq G^0$ (such a $U$
  exists because $G^0_{\red}$ is reduced and $\overline k$ is
  algebraically closed). For any $\overline k$-point $h \in G^0$ the
  translate $hg^{-1} U$ (for $g \in U(\overline k)$) is a smooth open
  neighbourhood of $h$, so $G^0$ is locally irreducible at every closed
  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
  6.1.10). The image $\alpha(U \times U) = G^0$ is the surjective image
  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme $U \times U$, so $G^0$ is
  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
  $G$, this makes $G^0$ of finite type over $k$.
\end{proof}

\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
\label{sec:pic0_definition}

We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
$G = \Pic_{C/k}$.

\begin{definition}[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
  \uses{def:identity_component_group_scheme, def:pic_scheme}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
  % L2921-L2929).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$, we now
  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
  % $\IPicz_{X/S}$ of the connected components of the identity element,
  % $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$, for $s\in S$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
  %   Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.  Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$
  % exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$.  Then $\IPic_{X/k}$ is
  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
  % $\IPicz_{X/k}$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
  % $k$.
  %  \end{prp}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
  $k$. The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
  $\Picz_{C/k}$ is the identity component
  $(\Pic_{C/k})^0$ of the $k$-group scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ of
  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, $\Picz_{C/k}$ is an open
  and closed subgroup scheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$ of finite type over $k$,
  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
  extension of the base field.
\end{definition}

\section{The degree map}
\label{sec:pic_degree}

The disjoint-union structure of $\Pic_{C/k}$
(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
quasi-projective $k$-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
$\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$ of a representing invertible sheaf
(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
integral curve $C/k$ of genus $g$ with a fixed degree-one polarisation
$\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on $C$, the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
sheaf $\mathcal{L}$ on $C$ is, by Riemann--Roch,
$\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g$, so it is
linear in $n$ with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
$\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)$ with the integers, the index being the
degree.

\begin{definition}[Degree of a Picard class]
  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
  \uses{def:pic_scheme, def:hilbert_polynomial}
  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve over $k$. The degree of a
  % divisor $D = n_i P_i$ on $C$ is $n_i [k(P_i):k]$. Since every
  % invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}$ on $C$ is of the form $\mathcal{L}(D)$
  % for some divisor $D$, and $D$ is uniquely determined up to linear
  % equivalence, we can define $\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)$. Then
  % $\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)$, and the Riemann-Roch
  % theorem says that $\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g$.
  % This gives a more canonical description of $\deg(\mathcal{L})$: when
  % $\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)$ is written as a polynomial in $n$,
  % $\deg(\mathcal{L})$ is the leading coefficient. We write
  % $\Pic^0(C)$ for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
  % of degree zero on $C$."
  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
  \S III.1, p.~88.}
  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
  $g$, and let $\Pic_{C/k}$ be the Picard scheme of
  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
  \[
    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
  \]
  is defined as follows: a $k$-point $\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)$
  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
  $\mathcal{L}$ on $C$; its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
  degree-one polarisation $\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on $C$ is, by
  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
  \[
    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
  \]
  a polynomial in $n$ linear in $n$. The integer
  $\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}$ is the leading coefficient of
  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
  $[\mathcal{L}]$ and (because $\Pic_{C/k}$ represents the
  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the $k$-point
  $\lambda$. The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
  $\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}$ invertible sheaves on $C$ one has
  $\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}$,
  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
  twists. Equivalently, the degree on $k$-points is the index of the
  connected component of $\Pic_{C/k}$ containing $\lambda$ under the
  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
  $\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}$ is indexed by
  the degree, with $\Pic^d_{C/k}$ the component representing classes
  of degree $d$.
\end{definition}

\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}

The terminal statement of the chapter identifies $\Picz_{C/k}$ with
an abelian variety of dimension $g$ --- the Jacobian variety of $C$.
Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a $k$-group scheme is an
\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
variety over $k$; commutativity and projectivity then follow
automatically.

\begin{theorem}[$\Picz_{C/k}$ is an abelian variety of dimension $g$]
  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
        def:divisor_degree_pic, def:genus, thm:fga_pic_representability}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
  % L3990-L3996).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
  % Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
  % curves of arithmetic genus $p_a$, and $S$ is Noetherian.  Show the
  % ``generalized Jacobians'' $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ form a smooth
  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension $p_a$.  And show this
  % family is projective if and only if $X/S$ is smooth.
  % \end{ex}"
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
  %  Assume $X/S$ is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
  % connected curves of genus $g>0$, and $S$ is Noetherian.  Set
  % $J:=\IPicz_{X/S}$; it exists and is a projective Abelian $S$-scheme by
  % Exercise~\ref{ex:jac}."
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8) and Rmk.~III.1.4(e),
  p.~86 (dimension equals genus).}
  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
  $g$ over a field $k$. Then the identity component
  $\Picz_{C/k}$ of \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an
  \emph{abelian variety over $k$}: a smooth, proper, geometrically
  irreducible commutative $k$-group scheme. Its dimension is
  \[
    \dim_k \Picz_{C/k} \;=\; g \;=\; g(C),
  \]
  the genus of $C$ (\cref{def:genus}). Its $k$-points are exactly the
  classes of invertible sheaves of degree zero:
  $\Picz_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)$, the kernel of the degree map of
  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
% identity-component substrate.)
% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of $\IPicz_{X/k}$:)
% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
%  Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.  Assume $X/k$ is projective
% and geometrically integral.  Then $\IPicz_{X/k}$ exists and is
% quasi-projective.  If also $X/k$ is geometrically normal, then
% $\IPicz_{X/k}$ is projective."
% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals $h^1(\mathcal O)$ when smooth:)
% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field
% $k$. Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\et}$.  Then
% $$\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).$$
%  Equality holds if and only if $\IPic_{X/k}$ is smooth at $0;$ if so, then
% $\IPic_{X/k}$ is smooth of dimension $\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})$
% everywhere."
% (from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86:)
% "(e) The dimension of $J$ is the genus of $C$. If $C$ has genus zero,
% then $\Jac(C) = 0$ (this is obvious, because $\Pic^0(C) = 0$, even
% when one goes to the algebraic closure). If $C$ has genus 1, then
% $\Jac(C) = C$ (provided $C$ has a rational point; otherwise it differs
% from $C$ --- because $\Jac(C)$ always has a point)."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
        thm:fga_pic_representability, def:divisor_degree_pic, def:genus}
  We assemble the conclusion from four inputs.

  \emph{Open and closed subgroup of finite type, geometrically
  irreducible.} By
  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
  $G = \Pic_{C/k}$ (a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type by
  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
  $\Picz_{C/k}$ is an open and closed subgroup scheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$,
  of finite type over $k$, geometrically irreducible, and its
  formation commutes with extending $k$. In particular $\Picz_{C/k}$ is
  separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$,
  and connected: it is a complete subscheme \emph{candidate} for the
  abelian-variety conclusion.

  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
  $\Pic_{C/k}$ (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
  of open quasi-projective $k$-subschemes) restricted to $\Picz_{C/k}$
  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective $k$-scheme
  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: $X/k$ projective and geometrically
  integral $\Longrightarrow$ $\Picz_{X/k}$ is quasi-projective).

  \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper geometrically
  integral curve $C/k$ of genus $g > 0$, $C/k$ is also geometrically
  normal (the smoothness implies geometric regularity, hence geometric
  normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p then upgrades the
  quasi-projective conclusion to projective: $\Picz_{C/k}$ is
  projective over $k$, in particular proper. The proof of th:qpp\&p
  uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling
  out non-constant maps $\mathbb G_m \to \Picz_{C/k}$, which on a
  geometrically normal $X$ becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.

  \emph{Smoothness and dimension.} By Kleiman~\S 5, Cor.~cor:sm,
  $\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g$
  (\cref{def:genus}), with equality if and only if $\Pic_{C/k}$ is
  smooth at $0$, in which case it is smooth of dimension $g$
  everywhere. For a smooth proper curve $C/k$, the Picard scheme is
  smooth at $0$: this is Exercise ex:jac of Kleiman~\S 5, which
  asserts that for $X/S$ projective and flat with integral geometric
  fibres of arithmetic genus $p_a$, the generalised Jacobians
  $\Picz_{X_s/k_s}$ form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative
  dimension $p_a$; specialising to $S = \Spec k$ and $X = C$ a smooth
  proper curve of (geometric = arithmetic) genus $g$ gives
  smoothness and dimension $g$ for $\Picz_{C/k}$.

  Combining all four steps: $\Picz_{C/k}$ is a smooth, projective,
  geometrically irreducible $k$-group scheme of dimension $g$. A
  proper connected $k$-group variety is by definition an
  \emph{abelian variety} (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically
  commutative (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Hence $\Picz_{C/k}$ is an
  abelian variety of dimension $g$.

  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map.} A class
  $\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)$ lies in the connected component of the
  identity precisely when the Hilbert polynomial of a representing
  invertible sheaf coincides with that of $\mathcal{O}_C$ ---
  equivalently, when its degree (the leading coefficient of the
  Hilbert polynomial of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
  $\Picz_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb Z)$.
\end{proof}

\section{Lean encoding}
\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}

The Lean target file is
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
    of a $k$-group scheme $G$ locally of finite type. The construction
    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is the bundled
    statement that the identity component is an open and closed
    subgroup scheme of finite type over $k$, geometrically irreducible,
    and that its formation commutes with extension of the base field
    --- the four conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
    $\Pic_{C/k}$ obtained by applying the
    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
    $G = \Pic_{C/k}$, inheriting the $k$-group-scheme structure from
    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
    $\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}$ extracting the leading coefficient
    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation $\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on
    $C$. On the disjoint-union decomposition of
    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
    component.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
    abelian-variety instance on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: smooth, proper,
    geometrically irreducible $k$-group scheme of dimension $g(C)$,
    with $k$-points the degree-zero classes.
\end{itemize}

The abstract identity-component substrate
(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} +
\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}) is new project
material: it is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is
owed in a follow-up iteration. The downstream
\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety} consumes Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm
and Ex.~ex:jac, both of which currently live only at the blueprint
level; their Lean specialisation to the smooth-proper-curve case is
the main substantive step of A.3.

\section{Out of scope}
\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}

This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
$\Picz_{C/k}$ as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$,
its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of $\Picz_{C/k}$}
    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of $f_P : C \to \Picz_{C/k}$
    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
  \item \textbf{The autoduality $\Picz_{C/k} \cong (\Picz_{C/k})^\vee$}
    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
  \item \textbf{The torsion component $\Pic^\tau_{C/k}$}
    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
    $\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Picz_{C/k}$, so the finer torsion-component
    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
    Route~A.
  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
    schemes $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k$.} This chapter
    defines the degree on $k$-points only; the upgrade to a
    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
    $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k$) is the natural assembly statement but
    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
\end{itemize}

\section{Internal-consistency check}
\label{sec:pic0_consistency_check}

The five declaration blocks form a connected $\uses$ chain rooted in
the sibling chapters \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability},
\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, and \cref{chap:Genus}:

  \item \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} is a structural
    Archon-internal abstract substrate; no cross-chapter
    \texttt{\textbackslash uses} pointers.
  \item \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} uses

  \item \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} uses
    \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} and
    \cref{def:pic_scheme} (from
    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
  \item \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} uses \cref{def:pic_scheme}
    (from \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) and
    \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} (from \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}).
  \item \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety} uses
    \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme},
    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup},
    \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic},
    \cref{def:genus} (from \cref{chap:Genus}), and
    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} (from
    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
\end{itemize}

The cross-chapter labels \texttt{def:pic\_scheme},
